reset_tests.description: No reset port exists. Verify there is no hidden initialization or stored state by checking that gray is purely a function of the current bin value from time 0 onward.

vectors.check: Startup value must not depend on prior history
Change input after startup and confirm no state retention
Returning to an earlier input must immediately restore the same output

normal_tests.description: Exhaustive functional test over all 1024 binary input combinations using oracle gray = bin ^ (bin >> 1). This is preferred because the module is small combinational logic with only 10 effective input bits.

vectors.check: For every input, gray[9] = bin[9] and gray[i] = bin[i+1] ^ bin[i] for i=0..8

normal_tests.description: Representative basic vectors that make the formula obvious and exercise typical bit transitions.
Single-hot walking-1 test to verify each output bit depends on the correct adjacent input bits and to catch omitted or constant-tied outputs.

corner_case_tests.description: Boundary values and alternating patterns to catch wrong shift direction, MSB errors, XNOR substitutions, and partial assignments.

vectors.target_bug: All-one boundary, MSB copy, lower-bit XOR correctness
Alternating pattern exposes XNOR and adjacency mistakes

corner_case_tests.description: Directed vectors that distinguish correct right shift from incorrect left shift implementation.
Bit-boundary vectors for off-by-one loop/index errors, especially around gray[0], gray[8], and gray[9].

vectors.target_bug: gray[9] copied directly, not XORed or forced
Upper boundary with MSB=0 and all lower bits=1

corner_case_tests.description: Walking-0 patterns on an all-ones background to catch stale constants and wrong neighboring-bit selection.

sequence_tests.description: Combinational responsiveness sequence: apply a changing stream of inputs without any clock and verify gray updates after each change with no one-cycle-late, registered, or latched behavior.

Monotonic count sequence over consecutive binary values to verify Gray-code adjacency behavior: consecutive inputs should produce outputs consistent with gray = bin ^ (bin >> 1), and for true Gray encoding adjacent numeric inputs differ by exactly one output bit.

vectors.expected_rule: For each sample, gray = bin ^ (bin >> 1); between successive samples, Hamming distance(gray[n], gray[n+1]) = 1

random_tests.description: Apply 200 random 10-bit bin values and compute expected_gray with the independent oracle gray = bin ^ (bin >> 1). Include some back-to-back random changes and repeated values to catch hidden state, latching, stale outputs, and accidental clocked behavior.

key_discriminators: Exhaustively enumerate all 1024 possible 10-bit inputs; this alone should strongly separate the correct implementation from wiring, index, width, and XNOR mutants.
gray[9] must always equal bin[9]; testing bin values with MSB=1 and MSB=0 isolates incorrect MSB handling.
Each lower bit must satisfy gray[i] = bin[i+1] ^ bin[i]; walking-1 and walking-0 patterns expose wrong adjacent-bit selection and off-by-one indexing.
Use vectors such as bin=10'b0000000001 and bin=10'b1000000000 to distinguish the correct right-shift formula from an incorrect left-shift implementation.
Alternating patterns 10'b1010101010 and 10'b0101010101 are highly discriminative for XOR vs XNOR and for omitted/tied output bits.
Back-to-back input changes without any clock must immediately change gray; this separates correct combinational logic from registered or latched implementations.
Repeatedly revisiting the same input after other values should always reproduce the same gray output, ruling out stale state or history dependence.
Consecutive binary counts should produce Gray outputs matching the formula, and adjacent counts should differ by exactly one output bit, which catches subtle bit-slice and formula errors.
